## About the Chimp Memory Test

The Chimp Test is inspired by one of the most famous experiments in cognitive psychology. In 2007, researchers at Kyoto University's Primate Research Institute demonstrated that young chimpanzees possessed extraordinary working memory for spatial-numerical associations.

### The Famous Study

In the original experiment, chimpanzees were shown numbers 1-9 briefly displayed on a touchscreen. After the numbers disappeared (replaced by white squares), the chimps had to touch the squares in numerical order. Remarkably, the chimps could remember all 9 positions with high accuracy, even when the numbers were displayed for just 210 milliseconds.

### Human vs. Chimp Performance

When the same test was given to human adults, performance was significantly worse. Humans typically struggled to remember more than 5-6 positions. This finding challenged assumptions about human cognitive superiority and highlighted the incredible working memory capabilities of our primate cousins.

### What This Test Reveals

The Chimp Test measures the capacity and precision of your visuospatial working memory — the ability to briefly hold and manipulate visual information. It's a purer measure of working memory than many other tests because it relies minimally on language or learned strategies.

## About the Color Perception Test

The Color Perception Test challenges your ability to discriminate between similar colors. This skill, known as color discrimination or hue discrimination, is an important aspect of visual perception that varies significantly between individuals.

### The Science of Color Vision

Human color vision is made possible by cone cells in the retina — photoreceptors sensitive to different wavelengths of light. Most people have three types of cones (trichromats), sensitive to short (blue), medium (green), and long (red) wavelengths.

The relative sensitivity of these cones varies between individuals, which is why some people are better at distinguishing certain color ranges than others. Women typically have better color discrimination than men, and some women may even have four types of cones (tetrachromats), giving them exceptional color vision.

### How Our Test Works

Each round presents you with a target color and four similar options. The options are created by slightly varying the hue, saturation, and lightness of the target color. Only one option matches exactly — the others are subtly different.

The test becomes more challenging as the similar colors get closer to the target. Scoring well requires careful attention to subtle color differences.

### Why Color Perception Matters

Good color discrimination is valuable in many professions and activities. Graphic designers, photographers, painters, and interior designers all rely on precise color perception. Even in everyday life, being able to distinguish subtle color differences helps with everything from choosing ripe produce to coordinating outfits.

### Improving Your Color Perception

Like many perceptual skills, color discrimination can improve with practice. Spending time consciously comparing colors, learning color theory, and regularly challenging yourself with tests like this one can sharpen your color acuity over time. Many artists report that their color perception improved significantly during their training.

## About Dual N-Back

The Dual N-Back task was developed by researchers seeking to push the boundaries of working memory training. Unlike simpler memory tasks, Dual N-Back requires you to simultaneously track two independent streams of information — a spatial position and an auditory/visual letter.

### The Controversial Study

In 2008, a study by Jaeggi and colleagues claimed that training with Dual N-Back could increase fluid intelligence — the ability to solve novel problems independently of acquired knowledge. The study generated enormous interest and controversy. While subsequent research has produced mixed results, Dual N-Back remains one of the most studied and respected cognitive training paradigms.

### How It Challenges Your Brain

Dual N-Back is demanding because it requires continuous updating of working memory. You must constantly add new information while discarding old information, all while comparing current stimuli to what you saw N steps ago. This "mental juggling" exercises the central executive component of working memory more intensely than almost any other cognitive task.

## About Hangman

Hangman is one of the oldest and most widely played word games in the world. Its origins date back to Victorian England, where it was known as "Birds, Beasts, and Fishes." The game has been a classroom staple for generations, used by teachers to make vocabulary practice fun.

### The Classic Formula

The premise is simple: guess letters to reveal a hidden word. Each wrong guess brings the hangman closer to completion. The tension builds with each wrong letter, creating a compelling risk-reward dynamic. The game is universally understood and requires no complex rules — just a vocabulary and a willingness to guess.

### Cognitive Benefits

Hangman exercises multiple cognitive skills: vocabulary recall, pattern recognition, probabilistic reasoning, and working memory. Players must track which letters have been used, consider letter frequency, and use contextual hints to narrow down possibilities. It's a deceptively rich cognitive exercise disguised as a simple game.

## About IQ Puzzles

Pattern recognition is fundamental to human intelligence. From recognizing faces to understanding language, our brains are constantly identifying patterns in the world around us. IQ-style matrix puzzles are designed to measure this core cognitive ability.

### The Psychology of Pattern Recognition

Matrix reasoning tasks are a key component of many intelligence tests because they measure fluid intelligence — the ability to solve novel problems independent of acquired knowledge. These tasks require you to identify abstract relationships and apply them to new situations.

### How These Puzzles Work

## About the Memory Card Game

The Memory Card Game, also known as Concentration or Pairs, is one of the most popular cognitive training games worldwide. It tests and trains your visual-spatial working memory — the ability to temporarily hold and manipulate visual information in your mind.

### The Cognitive Benefits

Research has shown that regular practice with memory matching games can improve working memory capacity, attention span, and visual discrimination skills. These cognitive abilities are essential for everyday tasks like remembering where you placed your keys, recognizing faces, and following directions.

Working memory is often called the "workbench of the mind" — it's where you hold and process information in the moment. Strong working memory is correlated with better academic performance, problem-solving ability, and even fluid intelligence.

### How Scoring Works

Your score is calculated based on two factors: the number of moves you make and the time you take. Fewer moves and less time result in a higher score. The formula rewards efficiency — finding pairs with fewer flips is more important than raw speed.

The minimum possible score is 8 moves (one flip per pair, getting every match on the first try). While this is theoretically possible, it requires perfect memory and a bit of luck!

### Memory Techniques to Try

Different people use different memory strategies. Some prefer a visual approach — creating a mental image of the card grid. Others use spatial memory — remembering where each card is located relative to others. Experiment with different techniques to find what works best for you.

The most effective strategy is active recall: instead of passively looking at cards, actively try to remember them before you need to. When you flip a card, immediately try to recall where you've seen its match before.

## About Mental Rotation

Mental rotation is one of the most studied abilities in cognitive psychology. Pioneering research by Shepard and Metzler in 1971 showed that people mentally rotate objects at a roughly constant speed — the more rotation required, the longer it takes to decide if two shapes match.

### The Classic Experiment

Shepard and Metzler showed participants pairs of 3D block figures and asked if they were the same shape. The key finding: response time increased linearly with the angle of rotation between the two shapes. This suggested that people were performing an analog mental rotation — imagining the shape turning in their mind — rather than using a purely analytical strategy.

### Why It's Important

Spatial reasoning — including mental rotation — is a distinct form of intelligence that predicts success in STEM fields. Engineers, surgeons, pilots, and architects all rely heavily on spatial reasoning skills. Research has shown that spatial ability is a better predictor of STEM achievement than math or verbal ability alone.

What is the average digit span?

The average person can remember 7 ± 2 digits in short-term memory. This is known as Miller's Law. Most people can reliably recall 5-9 digits. Remembering 10+ digits is considered excellent, and 15+ digits is exceptional.

Digit span is a standard psychological test used to measure short-term and working memory capacity. It's part of many intelligence tests including the WAIS (Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale). The test measures how many digits you can hold in your mind and accurately recall.
Can I improve my digit span?

Yes! With practice, you can improve your digit span. Techniques like chunking (grouping digits into meaningful units), creating associations, and using the method of loci (memory palace) can significantly increase your digit memory. Regular practice is key.
Why is my digit span different from the average?

Digit span varies naturally between individuals and can be affected by factors like age, stress, fatigue, and attention level. Even within the same person, digit span can vary from day to day. Focus on your personal improvement trend rather than comparing to averages.

## About the Number Memory Test

The Number Memory Test is based on the digit span task, one of the oldest and most widely used measures of short-term memory in cognitive psychology. First developed in the 19th century, digit span testing remains a core component of modern intelligence and memory assessments.

### The Psychology Behind Digit Span

Your short-term memory (also called working memory) is like a mental workspace where you temporarily hold information. For most people, this workspace can hold about 7 items at once. When you try to remember a sequence of digits, each digit takes up one "slot" in this workspace.

As the number gets longer, it exceeds your natural capacity, and you need to use strategies like chunking to remember more. Chunking involves grouping individual digits into larger, more meaningful units. For example, instead of remembering "1-4-9-2" as four separate digits, you might remember "1492" as the year Columbus sailed to America.

### How Our Test Works

Our test starts with a 3-digit number and increases by one digit each time you answer correctly. You have a limited time to view the number — longer numbers get more viewing time, but the challenge increases as the number grows.

The number disappears automatically, and you must type it from memory. There's no time limit for typing your answer, so you can focus entirely on accurate recall.

### What Your Results Mean

- **3-5 digits**: Below average short-term memory span
- **6-8 digits**: Average — in line with most people
- **9-12 digits**: Above average — good working memory
- **13-16 digits**: Excellent — exceptional short-term memory
- **17+ digits**: Superior — world-class digit memory

### The Real-World Importance of Digit Memory

While remembering long strings of numbers might seem like a party trick, digit span is actually correlated with many important cognitive abilities. Research has shown that working memory capacity (measured by tests like digit span) predicts academic achievement, language learning ability, and even job performance in complex roles.

Psychologically, verifying an answer is different from generating one. When you verify, you can use estimation, rounding, and pattern recognition — skills that are often faster and more practical than exact calculation. ## About the Reaction Time Test

The Reaction Time Test is a simple but powerful tool for measuring your visual-motor response speed. It's based on the same principles used in cognitive psychology research and sports science to measure processing speed.

### The Science of Reaction Time

Reaction time is the interval between the presentation of a stimulus and your response to it. It involves several stages of neural processing: your eyes detect the color change, this signal travels to your brain's visual cortex, your brain processes the information and decides to act, and finally a motor signal is sent to your finger to click.

This entire process typically takes 200-250 milliseconds in healthy adults. The fastest possible human reaction time is around 100-120ms, limited by the speed of neural transmission.

### What Your Results Mean

- **100-150ms**: Exceptional — competitive gamer or elite athlete level
- **150-200ms**: Excellent — well above average
- **200-250ms**: Average — typical for healthy adults
- **250-300ms**: Below average — may improve with practice
- **300ms+**: Slow — could indicate fatigue or distraction

### Why Reaction Time Matters

Quick reaction time is crucial in many real-world situations, from driving safely to excelling in sports and video games. Regular testing can help you monitor your cognitive health — a sudden decline in reaction time could indicate fatigue, stress, or other health issues worth addressing.

### How to Get the Most Accurate Results

For the most consistent measurements, take the test at the same time each day, in the same environment. Use a mouse rather than a touchpad for more precise timing. Avoid testing when you're tired, hungry, or distracted. Your first few attempts may be slower as you get used to the test — this is normal.

Sequence memory is a fundamental cognitive ability that underlies many everyday activities. From remembering a phone number long enough to dial it, to following a recipe step by step, sequence memory is constantly at work.

### The Science of Sequential Memory

Sequential memory relies on the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad — two components of working memory identified by cognitive psychologists. The phonological loop handles verbal sequences (like numbers or words), while the visuospatial sketchpad handles spatial sequences (like positions or movements).

As you succeed, the sequence grows longer, pushing the limits of your working memory capacity. The test ends when you make a mistake, giving you a clear measure of your current capacity.

### Real-World Applications

Strong sequence memory helps with learning new skills, following multi-step instructions, and even social interactions (remembering the flow of a conversation). Musicians, dancers, and athletes often have exceptional sequence memory from years of practice.

## About the Stroop Test

The Stroop effect, discovered by John Ridley Stroop in 1935, is one of the most famous and widely replicated findings in cognitive psychology. It reveals a fundamental truth about how our brains process information: automatic processes (like reading) can interfere with controlled processes (like naming colors).

### The Classic Experiment

In Stroop's original experiment, participants were shown color words printed in incongruent ink colors (e.g., "RED" printed in blue ink). When asked to name the ink color, participants were significantly slower and made more errors compared to naming the ink color of neutral stimuli. This interference occurs because reading is so automatic that we can't easily suppress it.

### What It Measures

The Stroop test measures cognitive control — your brain's ability to override an automatic response in favor of a less automatic one. This ability is a key component of executive function and is associated with the prefrontal cortex. Strong Stroop performance correlates with better self-control, decision-making, and focus in daily life.

What are the rules of Tower of Hanoi?

Move all disks from the left peg to the right peg. You can only move one disk at a time. A larger disk cannot be placed on top of a smaller disk. Click a peg to pick up the top disk, then click another peg to place it.
What's the minimum number of moves?
The minimum moves required is 2^n - 1, where n is the number of disks. For 3 disks: 7 moves. 4 disks: 15 moves. 5 disks: 31 moves. 6 disks: 63 moves.

For an even number of disks, make the first move to the middle peg. For odd, move to the right peg. The recursive pattern: move n-1 disks to the auxiliary peg, move the largest disk, then move n-1 disks to the target.

## About Tower of Hanoi

The Tower of Hanoi was invented in 1883 by French mathematician Édouard Lucas. The puzzle was accompanied by a legend: in a temple in Benares, priests were moving 64 golden disks according to the same rules. When they finish, the world will end. At one move per second, that would take about 585 billion years.

### Mathematical Elegance

The Tower of Hanoi is a perfect demonstration of recursive thinking. The solution for n disks is elegantly simple: move n-1 disks to the auxiliary peg, move the largest disk to the target, then move n-1 disks to the target. This recursive structure makes it a classic example in computer science education.

What is a good typing speed?

The average typing speed is around 40 WPM. A good typing speed is 60-80 WPM, which puts you above the average. Professional typists and competitive typists can reach speeds of 100+ WPM. For most office jobs, 50-60 WPM is considered acceptable.

WPM (Words Per Minute) is calculated by dividing the total number of characters typed correctly by 5 (the average word length in English), then dividing by the time in minutes. Errors are typically deducted from your score. Some tests calculate 'net WPM' by subtracting errors from the total, while others use 'gross WPM' which counts all characters typed.
Can I improve my typing speed?

Yes! Regular practice is the key to improving your typing speed. Focus on accuracy first — speed will follow naturally. Practice for 15-30 minutes daily for the best results. Using proper finger placement (touch typing) and maintaining good posture will also help significantly.
Why is my typing speed different on different tests?

Different typing tests use different text passages with varying difficulty levels. Some texts contain more complex words, punctuation, or capitalization. The length of the test also matters — shorter tests may show higher WPM. For the most accurate measure, take multiple tests and average the results.

Absolutely! Touch typing (typing without looking at the keyboard) can significantly improve your speed and accuracy. It also reduces eye strain and neck pain from constantly looking down at the keyboard. Most people can learn touch typing in 2-3 weeks with consistent practice.

## About the Typing Speed Test

The Typing Speed Test is one of the most practical and widely-used cognitive tests available. Originally developed for clerical and secretarial job assessments, typing speed tests have evolved into a valuable tool for anyone looking to improve their keyboard efficiency.

### Why Typing Speed Matters

In today's digital world, typing is an essential skill. Whether you're a student taking notes, a professional writing emails, or a programmer writing code, faster and more accurate typing can save you hours each week. The average person spends 2-3 hours per day typing. Improving your WPM by just 20 words per minute could save you 30+ minutes every day.

### How Our Test Works

Our typing test presents you with a passage of text that you must type as accurately as possible. The test measures both your speed (in WPM) and your accuracy (as a percentage). Your final score is calculated as net WPM, which accounts for errors made during the test.

### What the Results Mean

- **0-30 WPM**: Below average — you would benefit from touch typing practice
- **30-45 WPM**: Average — typical for most casual computer users
- **45-60 WPM**: Above average — good for most office environments
- **60-80 WPM**: Excellent — faster than ~90% of the population
- **80-100+ WPM**: Exceptional — competitive typing speed

### Tips for Long-Term Improvement

The key to lasting improvement is consistent, deliberate practice. Rather than taking the test repeatedly in one session, spread your practice across multiple days. Your brain consolidates motor skills during sleep, so daily practice is far more effective than cramming.

Focus on accuracy over speed. Research shows that correcting errors slows you down more than typing at a slightly slower but more accurate pace. As your accuracy improves, your speed will naturally increase without conscious effort.

## About the Verbal Memory Test

Verbal memory is what allows us to remember conversations, learn new vocabulary, and recall facts we've read. It's the foundation of academic learning and everyday communication.

### Recognition vs. Recall

This test measures recognition memory (identifying whether you've seen something before) rather than recall memory (actively retrieving information from memory). Recognition is typically easier than recall — most people recognize far more words than they can actively produce. This is why multiple-choice tests feel easier than fill-in-the-blank.

### The Neuroscience of Word Memory

Word recognition involves the brain's language network, including Broca's area (speech production), Wernicke's area (language comprehension), and the hippocampus (memory formation). The more associations you create with a word, the stronger the memory trace becomes.

### Practical Benefits

Strong verbal memory helps with learning new languages, remembering names, and absorbing information from reading. Students, professionals who work with text, and anyone learning new terminology can benefit from improved verbal memory skills.

## About the Visual Memory Test

Visual memory is one of the most important cognitive abilities we use daily. From remembering where you parked your car to recognizing a friend's face in a crowd, visual-spatial memory is constantly at work.

### How This Test Works

The Visual Memory Test presents you with a grid of tiles. In each round, several tiles light up briefly. Your task is to remember which tiles were highlighted and click them after they fade. As you progress, more tiles are highlighted, making the challenge increasingly difficult.

### The Science Behind Visual Memory

Visual-spatial working memory is processed primarily in the right hemisphere of the brain, involving the parietal and occipital lobes. Research has shown that this type of memory can be improved through regular practice, a phenomenon known as cognitive training.

### Benefits of Strong Visual Memory

People with strong visual memory tend to excel at tasks involving spatial reasoning, such as navigation, architecture, and design. Visual memory is also important for reading comprehension, as it helps you remember the layout of text and diagrams on a page.

## About Word Scramble

Word scrambles (also called anagrams) have been a popular puzzle format for centuries. They appear in newspapers, puzzle books, and increasingly in brain training apps. The appeal is simple: unscrambling letters into a meaningful word is both challenging and satisfying.

### The Cognitive Challenge

Word scrambles engage multiple cognitive systems: visual pattern recognition (seeing letter combinations), verbal fluency (accessing your vocabulary), and working memory (holding and rearranging letters mentally). This multi-system engagement makes word scrambles excellent brain training exercises.

### Anagram Solving Strategies

Expert anagram solvers don't try every possible combination. Instead, they look for patterns: common letter pairs, likely word beginnings and endings, and the overall structure of the word. With practice, these strategies become automatic, and your solving speed improves dramatically.

### Vocabulary Benefits

Regular word puzzle solving expands your vocabulary and reinforces word knowledge. Each word you unscramble strengthens the neural pathways associated with that word, making it easier to access in the future. This is one reason why crossword puzzle enthusiasts often have impressive vocabularies.

## About Wordle

Wordle took the world by storm in late 2021. Created by software engineer Josh Wardle as a gift for his partner, the simple word-guessing game became a global phenomenon, eventually being acquired by The New York Times.

### The Appeal

Wordle's genius lies in its constraints: one puzzle per day, six guesses, five letters. These limits create a perfect daily ritual — short enough to do over coffee, challenging enough to feel rewarding. The color-coded feedback system is instantly intuitive, and the shareable results (without spoilers) fueled its viral spread on social media.

### Strategic Vocabulary Building

Beyond entertainment, Wordle is excellent vocabulary training. Regular play expands your active vocabulary, improves pattern recognition for letter combinations, and develops systematic problem-solving skills. The game exercises the same cognitive systems used in word puzzles, crosswords, and language learning.
